coredata.dc:title: Experimental study on suppression of thermal runaway propagation of lithium-ion battery by salt hydrate based dry powder extinguishants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiutao
Zhu, Yuxian
Du, Kang
Zhou, Xiaomeng
coredata.dc:description: To develop the high cooling effect, high fire extinguishing performance, non-conductive and low cost extinguishant for fighting lithium-ion battery (LIB) fire, seven salt hydrates with different amounts of crystal water were systematically studied as the dry powder extinguishants in this work. The results of the differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) measurement demonstrate that the highest endothermic enthalpies of the salt hydrates below 150 °C is larger than 800 kJ kg−1, which can effectively suppress the thermal runaway (TR) propagation between LIBs. Moreover, the heat absorption capacity of the salt hydrate can be enhanced by increasing the reaction entropy, which can be achieved by increasing the crystal water content. The electric breakdown voltages of the salt hydrates were ranged from 4 kV to 60 kV, which are mainly determined by the crystal water content and the melting point. Furthermore, the fire extinguishing performances of the salt hydrates have no obvious relationship with their heat absorption capacities, but are related to their chemical component. This work systematically studied the factors that affect the cooling effect, the fire extinguishing performance and the conductivity of the salt hydrates, which provides an essential basis for developing high performance salt hydrate based dry powder extinguishants in fighting LIB fire.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salt hydrate
Lithium-ion battery
Thermal runaway
Fire suppression
Phase change

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The advantages and disadvantages of different extinguishants for fighting LIB fire.
Setup of TR propagation suppression experiment [8].
Apparatus for 1 m3 scale fire extinguishing experiment: (a) schematic illustration figure, (b) real photo.
Ceramic cup containing electrodes used to induce voltage breakdown.

DSC curves of (a) CaCl2·6H2O, (b) Na2HPO4·12H2O, (c) MgSO4·7H2O, (d) Na3PO4·12H2O, (e) KAl(SO4)2·12H2O, (f) Na2B4O7·10H2O, (g) MgCl2·6H2O, and (h) the corresponding endothermic enthalpy distribution bar chart.
(a) Relationship between reaction enthalpies and reaction entropies, (b) relationship between reaction entropies and the number of crystal water.

Temperature evolutions for TR propagation suppression experiment with the extinguishants of (a) Blank, (b) Commercial dry powder, (c) Na2HPO4·12H2O, (d) MgSO4·7H2O, (e) Na3PO4·12H2O, (f) KAl(SO4)2·12H2O, (g) Na2B4O7·10H2O.
Schematic diagram of heat transfer in the process of thermal runaway propagation.
Distribution of the critical heat with different extinguishing agents.
(a) Extinguishing concentration of salt hydrates, (b) relationship between extinguishing concentration and endothermic enthalpy of salt hydrates.
(a) Dielectric breakdown voltages of the commercial dry powder and salt hydrate samples, (b) correlation between dielectric breakdown voltage and melting point.
Reaction equations of the salt hydrates during dehydration process.
Melting point and latent heat of the studied salt hydrates [43–45].
Thermodynamic parameters of the dehydration reactions of salt hydrates.
The calculated heat transfer of cell-1 during thermal runaway for different tests.

head.ce:title: Experimental study on suppression of thermal runaway propagation of lithium-ion battery by salt hydrate based dry powder extinguishants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Civil Aviation Thermal Hazards Prevention and Emergency Response, Civil Aviation University of China, Tianjin, 300300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Civil Aviation Thermal Hazards Prevention and Emergency Response

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Civil Aviation Thermal Hazards Prevention and Emergency Response, Civil Aviation University of China, Tianjin 300300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Safety Science and Engineering, Civil Aviation University of China, Tianjin, 300300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Safety Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Safety Science and Engineering, Civil Aviation University of China, Tianjin 300300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To develop the high cooling effect, high fire extinguishing performance, non-conductive and low cost extinguishant for fighting lithium-ion battery (LIB) fire, seven salt hydrates with different amounts of crystal water were systematically studied as the dry powder extinguishants in this work. The results of the differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) measurement demonstrate that the highest endothermic enthalpies of the salt hydrates below 150 °C is larger than 800 kJ kg−1, which can effectively suppress the thermal runaway (TR) propagation between LIBs. Moreover, the heat absorption capacity of the salt hydrate can be enhanced by increasing the reaction entropy, which can be achieved by increasing the crystal water content. The electric breakdown voltages of the salt hydrates were ranged from 4 kV to 60 kV, which are mainly determined by the crystal water content and the melting point. Furthermore, the fire extinguishing performances of the salt hydrates have no obvious relationship with their heat absorption capacities, but are related to their chemical component. This work systematically studied the factors that affect the cooling effect, the fire extinguishing performance and the conductivity of the salt hydrates, which provides an essential basis for developing high performance salt hydrate based dry powder extinguishants in fighting LIB fire.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

row.entry: Heat transferred from cell-1 to cell-2
Heat transferred from cell-1 to the heating rod
Heat dissipated by the extinguishing agent
Mass of cell-1 before thermal runaway
Residual mass of cell-1 after thermal runaway
Initial temperature of cell-1 at the beginning of thermal runaway
Start time of thermal runaway for cell-1
Start time of thermal runaway for cell-2, or the time when cell-2 reaches the maximum temperature
Time when cell-1 and heating rod reach the same temperature for the first time
Time when cell-1 and heating rod reach the same temperature for the second time
Heat flow transferred from cell-1 to cell-2

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the fire accidents caused by lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted great concerns with the widespread applications of LIBs in electric vehicles, electric airplanes, electric ships, electrical appliances, energy storages, and so on [1–7]. The LIB fires are very difficult to extinguish due to the flammable released gas during thermal runaway (TR), the exothermic reactions within the cell and the enclosed structure of battery. And the increasing energy density of LIBs makes this problem more severe.

According to the TR mechanism and LIB fire spread characteristics, the best way to extinguish LIBs fire is to quickly remove the energy released by the battery and lower the battery temperature to inhibit the exothermic reactions within the cell [8,9]. Among all the fire extinguishants, water has the highest heat absorbing ability (the latent heat of vaporization is up to 2260.0 kJ kg−1) [10]. Thus, the water-based fire extinguishants, including water mist, water sprinkler, water jet and foam, have been widely studied in fighting LIB fires [11–17]. These researches have proved that the water-based fire extinguishants can effectively prevent the battery reburning and TR propagation. However, their high electric conductivity can cause external short circuit of the batteries and lead to secondary accidents, which limits their applications in LIB fires [18]. The high boiling point chemical gas extinguishants also show certain cooling effect, for instance, dodecafluoro-2-methylpentan-3-one (Novec 1230) possesses a latent heat of vaporization of 88 kJ kg−1, which can prevent the battery reburning by using large dose [19–22]. While, due to the high cost and the production of corrosive and toxic gases in fire extinguishing process, chemical gas extinguishants are not suitable for the large-scale applications in fighting LIB fires [18,23]. The inert gases (such as N2, CO2 and IG 541) show poor cooling effect, which are rarely used as LIB fire extinguishants [24]. Dry powder extinguishants have been widely applied in various fire protection fields, such as building fire, industrial fire, forest fire, urban fire, vehicle fire etc., benefiting from their advantages of excellent extinguishing efficiency, good insulation, low price, low toxicity, easy storage, environmental friendliness and suitable for water deficient areas [25–29]. However, due to the lower cooling effect, it has been proved that the traditional dry powder extinguishants were failed to prevent re-ignition and TR propagation of LIBs [30–32]. From the above review, it can be seen that all the present commonly used extinguishants have different advantages and disadvantages in fighting LIB fires and not fully meet the requirement of the battery safety (as shown in Fig. 1 ). An ideal LIB fire extinguishant should exhibit high cooling effect, high extinguishing efficiency, insulation, environment friendly and low cost, which combines the advantages of water and dry powder fire extinguishing agents.

Salt hydrate is a kind of combination of inorganic salts and water forming a typical crystalline solid with a general formula of M i N j ·xH2O. As one of most important phase change materials (PCMs), salt hydrates have been widely studied in latent heat thermal energy storage systems and battery thermal management systems [33–42]. During the thermal decomposition, salt hydrates experience the processes of phase change from solid to aqueous solution, water evaporation and residual solid decomposition, which corresponds to solid-liquid phase change heat absorption, water evaporation heat absorption and decomposition heat absorption [8]. The above endothermic processes endow salt hydrates with the potential strong heat absorption ability. Moreover, the salt hydrates have the advantages of cheap raw material, abundant diversity, non-flammability and non-conductive property in solid-state. Therefore, the salt hydrates have the potential to become the ideal fire extinguishing agents for fighting LIB fires.

In this work, seven salt hydrates with different amount of crystal water were systematically studied. Their cooling abilities and electrical safety performances were scaled using a differential scanning calorimeter and a dielectric strength tester. Through the LIB fire extinguishing experiment, their TR propagation suppression abilities were further evaluated. The results revealed that the heat absorption capacities of the salt hydrates can be enhanced by increasing their reaction entropies, and the highest endothermic enthalpy of the salt hydrates below 150 °C is larger than 800 kJ kg−1, which can effectively suppress the TR propagation between LIBs. Furthermore, the electric breakdown voltages of the salt hydrates can be modulated by the crystal water content and melting point.

All reagents were commercially available and used directly after receipt without further purification. CaCl2·6H2O (99 wt %), Na2HPO4·12H2O (99 wt %), MgSO4·7H2O (99 wt %), Na3PO4·12H2O (99 wt %), KAl(SO4)2·12H2O (99 wt %), Na2B4O7·10H2O (99 wt %), MgCl2·6H2O (99 wt %) and hydrophobic silicon oxide (specific surface area
>
200 m2 g−1) were purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co. Ltd.. The commercial dry powder (90 wt % ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) was purchased from Chuangwei Firefighting Material Co. Ltd..

To increase the spray effect of the sample, the salt hydrate samples tested in TR propagation suppression experiment and fire extinguishing experiment were mixed with 5 % hydrophobic silicon oxide and refined by ball milling with planetary ball mill (F-P4000, FOCUCY). The ball milling time was 1 h and the frequency was 25 Hz.

A differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, DSC 3 METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) was used to measure the endothermic capacities of the samples. 5–10 mg sample was sealed in an aluminum pan and heated from 25 °C to 550 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 in a nitrogen atmosphere with a gas flow rate of 50 mL min−1.

Commercial 18,650-type LIBs (SAMSUNG SDI INR18650-35E) with a capacity of 3500 mAh and a voltage of 4.2 V were used for all tests. Before the experiment, all batteries were charged to the full state of charge with a constant current (1700 mA)-constant voltage (4.2 V) charging mode.

The experimental platform was constructed according to the previous work [8]. Specifically, the platform includes explosion proof box, driving gas system (nitrogen gas cylinder and pressure relief valve), a temperature measuring system (data recorder and thermocouple), a battery heating system (thermostat and heating rod), a powder tank, a nozzle, batteries, and a high-speed camera (Fig. 2 ). The heating rod, cell-1, and cell-2 were arranged closely in a row (as shown in the inset of Fig. 2). The heating rod is the same size as the 18,650-type LIB. The extinguishing nozzle was 8 cm above cell-1.

The experimental procedures include: first, 100 g of the powder sample was filled into a powder tank; second, cell-1 was heated by the heating rod at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 from room temperature to 300 °C; third, when cell-1 fell into the TR, the dry powder sample was released at a pressure of 0.5 MPa until all samples has been sprayed out and then the gas drive was shut down. The temperatures of the batteries and the heating rod were measured by K-type thermocouple and recorded with a data recorder (HIOKI LR8402-21). A high-speed camera was used to record the state of the LIBs during the experiment.

A bench scale experimental apparatus of confined space (1 m3) was built according to the previous works after a minor modification [25,28]. The experimental platform was mainly constructed with transparent confined space (1 m × 1 m × 1 m), oil pan, sprayer, powder tank, driving gas system (nitrogen) and high speed camera (Fig. 3 ).

The dry powder sample was filled into a tank with conical bottom. One intake tube connected to the driving system was inserted at 1 cm to the bottom. Another discharge tube linked to the sprayer was inserted at 1 cm to the top. The pressure of the compressed nitrogen was controlled by mass flowmeter and set to 0.5 MPa for every test. 150 mL n-heptane was used as fuel in a square oil pan (20 cm × 20 cm × 4 cm) for each test. It should be noted that the oil pan was placed near the edge of the box to prevent it from being directly sprayed by the fire extinguishing agent. The experimental steps are as follows: first, the oil pan is ignited and the fire lasts for 20 s. Then, the suppressant is completely discharged within about 10 s.

For every sample, the lowest powder consumption was measured by gradually reducing the sample mass in each test from 50 g to 10 g and the decrement was set as 2.5 g for every test. The lowest powder consumption was repeated three times and only when all three experiments have successfully extinguished the fire, the corresponding powder consumption was considered valid.

The electric breakdown voltage was measured using an automatic dielectric strength tester (ZIJJ-IV, Bozhilang Electrical Equipment Co., Ltd) with a measuring range of 0–80 kV and a limited current of 5 mA. Fig. 4 shows the setup of electrodes and sample cup. Two circular electrodes (φ = 25 mm) were interspersed into the sample cup with a dielectric gap of 45 mm. For each test, a 250 g sample was placed in the sample cup. The voltage was started from 0 kV with a voltage rising rate of 2.5 kV/s and stopped until getting the breakdown voltage. Every sample was tested three times, and the average value was taken as the final result.

The main sources of uncertainty for the experimental results were derived from the corresponding testing devices. As stated above, the temperature was measured by a K-type thermocouple with an uncertainty of ±0.5 °C. The uncertainties of DSC include: temperature uncertainty of ±0.1 °C and calorimetric sensitivity of 0.04 μW. The uncertainties of dielectric strength tester include: measurement accuracy of ±1.5 % and voltage distortion rate <5 %.

Up to now, there are hundreds of salt hydrates that have been studied for energy storage materials. But as an ideal fire extinguishing agent, more factors should be considered. Such as incombustibility, low oxidability, low reducibility, low corrosiveness, low toxicity, high melting point (>25 °C), high latent heat (>150 kJ kg−1) and low cost. Taking into account the above factors comprehensively, seven salt hydrates with different melting point from 29 °C to 117 °C were selected and studied in this work (Table 1 ).

The heat absorbing abilities of the salt hydrates were studied by DSC measurement in this work. As displayed in Fig. 5 a–g, all the samples presented multiple endothermic peaks at different temperatures, which are attributed to the phase change, water vaporization and thermal decomposition during the heating process. According to the reported works, the TR temperatures of LIBs is ca. 150–180 °C [46], thus, to effectively cool down the LIBs in TR and suppress TR propagation in LIB modules, the ideal endothermic temperature of the salt hydrates should be lower than 150 °C [8]. The endothermic enthalpies below and above 150 °C for all the salt hydrates were shown in Fig. 5h. It can be seen that the endothermic enthalpies of Na2HPO4·12H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, Na3PO4·12H2O, KAl(SO4)2·12H2O, and Na2B4O7·10H2O below 150 °C are all larger than 600 kJ kg−1, which indicates that these salt hydrates possess the high cooling potential as fire extinguishing agent in fighting LIB fire. Although MgCl2·6H2O has the highest total endothermic enthalpy, its endothermic enthalpy below 150 °C is the lowest (138.2 kJ kg−1), which demonstrates that MgCl2·6H2O is not suitable as fire extinguishing agent for LIB fire. The similar reason applies to CaCl2·6H2O. It should be noted that the endothermic enthalpies below 150 °C for the above salt hydrates not only include the latent heat of fusion, but also contain the latent heat of water vaporization. Therefore, to evaluate the heat absorbing abilities of the salt hydrates, except the latent heat of fusion, the amount of crystalline water should also be considered, because it determines the heat absorbed by water vaporization. The detailed discussion will be presented in the following part.

According to the reported thermal decomposition mechanism of salt hydrates [47–52], the endothermic enthalpies below 150 °C are mostly attributed to the phase change from solid to solution and the following water vaporization, which can be expressed by an overall dehydration reaction (Scheme 1 ).

To reveal the origin of the heat absorbing ability of salt hydrates, the thermodynamic parameters of the corresponding dehydration reaction under standard condition were analysized. The reaction enthalpy (
Δ r
H m ⊖
), reaction entropy (
Δ r
S m ⊖
) and Gibbs free energy change of reaction (
Δ r
G m ⊖
) can be calculated by Eqs. (1)–(3).
(1)
Δ r

Δ f

The standard enthalpies (
Δ f
H m ⊖
) and Gibbs energies (
Δ r
G m ⊖
) of formation, and entropies of the salt hydrates and the corresponding thermal decomposition products can be obtained from the reported works (as shown in Table S1). The calculated results were listed in Table 2 . As can be seen that the values of the reaction enthalpies are positive, which demonstrates that these dehydration reactions are all endothermic reactions, and this is consistent with the results of DSC measurement. Moreover, the reaction enthalpies and the reaction entropies have a positive linear relationship (Fig. 6 a). In other words, the endothermic enthalpy of the dehydration reaction of the salt hydrate increases with the increase of the corresponding reaction entropy. Furthermore, Fig. 6b shows that the reaction entropy is related to the amount of the crystal water of the salt hydrate, and the more the crystal water, the larger the reaction entropy. This can be understood from the characteristics of the reactants and products in dehydration reaction of the salt hydrates. Table S1 clearly shows that the entropies of the dehydrated salt are smaller than that of the corresponding salt hydrates, in other words, the formation of the dehydrated salt will not increase the reaction entropy. While, the transformation of the solid crystal water to the gaseous water will result in a large reaction entropy increase. This demonstrates that designing high crystal water content can effectively increase the reaction entropy, and increasing the reaction entropy can obviously enhance the heat absorption capacity of the salt hydrate.

The salt hydrates with endothermic enthalpies larger than 600 kJ kg−1 below 150 °C were further investigated by the TR propagation suppression experiment to test their LIB fire suppression abilities. In this experiment, one cell was heated to initiate thermal runaway, then the extinguishing agent was released to see whether it can suppress the thermal runway of the second cell. For the blank test (Fig. 7 a), the temperature of cell-1 reached to about 620 °C after TR, then about 50 s later, cell-2 also experienced TR and its temperature was up to 660 °C, which indicates that the TR propagation was occurred between cell-1 and cell-2. For the application of the commercial dry powder (Fig. 7b), although the triggering time for cell-2 to TR was extended to about 150 s, the TR propagation between cell-1 and cell-2 was finally occurred. According to the previous works, the commercial dry powder shows a certain heat absorbing capacity due to its thermal decomposition reaction at ca. 200 °C, but it can't absorb heat below 150 °C [8]. Therefore, it is not possible to quickly lower the cell temperature to below 150 °C, thereby suppressing TR propagation between cell-1 and cell-2. While, for the application of salt hydrates (Fig. 7c–g), the maximum temperature of cell-2 was reduced to 110 °C–130 °C, which is lower the TR temperature of LIBs. These results prove the important role of the cooling ability of extinguishing agent in suppressing TR propagation between LIBs.

The cooling effects of the salt hydrates were further quantitatively analysized according to the heat transfer process of cell-1 (Fig. 8 ) [53,54]. Generally, when cell-1 undergoes thermal runaway, it will generate a lot of heat (Q
ge ) due to the side reactions and internal short circuit. Part of the heat will be ejected to the environment with the venting materials (such as TR gas, electrolyte, and electrode materials) (Q
ejecta ). Due to the temperature gradient, part of the heat can be transferred from cell-1 to cell-2 (Q
trans-cell ) and heating rod (Q
trans-HR ). The application of the extinguishing agent also can dissipate part of the self-generated heat (Q
diss ). The remaining heat will be kept inside the cell-1 (Q
in ). According to the law of energy conservation, the sum of the above heats should equal to the self-generated heat of the thermal runaway cell (Eq. (4)).
(4)
Q
g e
=
Q
e j e c t a
+
Q
t r a n s − c e l l
+
Q
t r a n s − H R
+
Q
d i s s
+
Q
i n

In this work, the thermal runaway occurred in a short time and the temperature of cell-1 rapidly rose to the maximum temperature. The heat dissipation during this period can be ignored and the self-generated heat of cell-1 can be calculated by Eq. (5),
(5)
Q
g e
= c m
(
T max
−
T
s t a r t
)
where c is the heat capacity of cell-1 (1.1 kJ kg−1 K−1) [55], m is the mass of cell-1, T
max is the maximum temperature of cell-1 and T
start is the initial temperature of cell-1 at the beginning of thermal runaway. Accordingly, the mass loss correlated Q
ejecta can be obtained by Eq. (6), where m
tr is the residual mass of cell-1 after the thermal runaway.
(6)
Q
e j e c t a
=
m −
m
t r
m
Q
g e

Generally, the heat transfer between cell-1 and cell-2 is mainly through the heat conduction of the cell shell and the heat radiation of the fire. However, the duration of LIB fire was relatively short in this work, the heat transfer caused by fire was very small. Moreover, previous research has proved that fire has little influence on the TR propagation process [56]. Thus, the heat radiation of the fire can be ignored, and the heat transfer between cell-1 and cell-2 can be calculated by Eqs. (7)–(9),
(7)
Q
t r a n s − c e l l
=

t 2

−
T 2

δ

λ

A
c e l l
where t
1 and t
2 are the start time of thermal runaway of cell-1 and cell-2 (if cell-2 does not undergo thermal runaway, t
2 represents the time when cell-2 reaches the maximum temperature), q
cell is the heat flow transferred from cell-1 to cell-2, T
1 is the temperature of cell-1, T
2 is the temperature of cell-2. R
cell is the thermal resistance, λ
cell is the thermal conductivity of LIB, which is 4.32 W m-1·K−1 [57]. Heat transfer distance (δ
cell ) and heat transfer area (A
cell ) are 0.018 m and 9.75
×
10−5 m2, respectively.

where t

HR is the heat flow transferred from cell-1 to heating rod, T
1 is the temperatures of cell-1, T
HR is the temperatures of heating rod. R
HR is the thermal resistance, λ
HR is the thermal conductivity. For the heating rod composed with stainless steel, the corresponding thermal conductivity value is 14.75 W m-1·K−1 [57]. Due to the same size of battery and heating rod, they have the same heat transfer distance (δ
HR , 0.018 m) and heat transfer area (A
HR , 9.75

10−5 m2).

The remaining heat in cell-1 after thermal runaway can be obtained from Eq. (13), where T

)

The calculated heat transfer of cell-1 during TR for different tests is listed in Table 3 . Considering the inconsistency of batteries, the heat distribution was normalized and displayed in Fig. 9 . As can be seen that over 50 % heat generated by TR was brought away by the ejecta of venting in all tests. For the blank test, 8 % heat was dissipated and 20 % heat stayed in the cell. When the commercial dry powder was applied, 16 % heat was dissipated and 16 % heat stayed in the cell. Although the commercial dry powder has no heat absorbing ability below 150 °C, its thermal decomposition reaction at ca. 200 °C is an endothermic reaction, which can dissipate some heat from the high temperature LIB. While, the salt hydrates can dissipate more than 25 % heat and only 4 %–7 % heat stayed in cell-1 during fire extinguishing process. The high cooling effects of the salt hydrates are due to their high heat absorbing ability below 150 °C.

In addition to the cooling effect, the fire extinguishing performance is also one of the important characteristics of the LIB fire extinguishing agent. To evaluate the fire extinguishing performance of the salt hydrate based dry powders, a bench scale experiment in 1 m3 confined space was carried out. Fig. 10 shows that the fire extinguishing performances of the salt hydrates are lower than that of the commercial dry powder and there is no direct relationship between fire extinguishing concentration and endothermic enthalpy. In the fact, the fire extinguishing performances of the dry powders are determined by their chemical component. Generally, the component containing the elements of nitrogen, phosphorus, boron et al. will present an advanced chemical extinguishing effect in suppressing fires [58–60]. Therefore, the commercial dry powder with the main component of NH4H2PO4 showed the highest fire extinguishing performance. Na2B4O7·10H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O and Na3PO4·12H2O presented the medium fire extinguishing performances. MgSO4·7H2O and KAl(SO4)2·12H2O had the worst fire extinguishing performances.

The electrical safety performances of the salt hydrate based dry powders were evaluated by their dielectric breakdown voltages. Fig. 11 a shows that the commercial dry powder has the highest electric breakdown voltage. When the crystal water was induced to the salt hydrates, their electric breakdown voltages decreased. And the more the amount of the crystal water, the lower the electric breakdown voltage. Moreover, for the salt hydrates with the same crystal water content, their electric breakdown voltages are increased with their melting points (Fig. 11b). Nevertheless, the lowest electric breakdown voltage of these salt hydrates is still larger than 4 kV, which is higher than the operating voltages of battery pack for the present electric vehicles (0.3 kV–0.8 kV) [61–65].

To develop the high cooling effect, non-conductive and low cost extinguishant for fighting LIB fire, seven salt hydrates with different amounts of crystal water were systematically studied in this work. The main results can be summarized as follow:
(1)
Increase the crystal water content can effectively increase the reaction entropy and increasing the reaction entropy can obviously enhance the heat absorption capacity of the salt hydrate.
(2)
The high heat absorbing salt hydrates can effectively suppress the TR propagation between LIBs. And the salt hydrates with high crystal water content show the best cooling effect.
(3)
Salt hydrates containing the elements of phosphorus and boron showed higher fire extinguishing performances than others.
(4)
Increasing the crystal water content can lower the electric breakdown voltage of the salt hydrates and, for the same crystal water content, the electric breakdown voltages are increased with their melting points of the salt hydrates.
In the future research, new salt hydrate with high reaction entropy and more chemical extinguishing elements should be designed to both achieve the better fire extinguishing and cooling performances.

Xiutao Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuxian Zhu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Kang Du: Investigation, Resources. Xiaomeng Zhou: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2233202) and Graduate Research Innovation Program of CAUC (2023YJSKC09017).
